1:
The witnesses set the scene with care, and their care is itself a lesson in timing. Wesley and Gill both marvel that Samuel chose this precise hour — Gilgal, just after the victory over Nahash, with Saul now confirmed and the people 'in the midst of all their mirth and jollity' — to begin a rebuke. It was, Wesley says, a stroke of 'great wisdom and integrity': had Samuel reproved the nation for demanding a king while Saul was still unsettled, their 'accustomed levity' might have made them 'as hastily cast off their king, as they had passionately desired him.' So the prophet waits until the throne is secure, then moves 'to temper their joys, and to excite them to that repentance which he saw wanting in them.'

The grammatical-historical readers frame the constitutional weight of the moment. Keil and Delitzsch see Samuel formally 'handing over the office of judge, which he had hitherto filled, to the king,' with the sober warning that Israel's prosperity had always hung on both people and ruler keeping 'that proper attitude towards the Lord.' Ellicott is the most lyrical: here, he believes, we possess 'many of the very words spoken on this momentous occasion,' treasured 'with a jealous care,' for Samuel was 'the link between the judges and the kings' — joining Eleazar, Gideon and Jephthah, 'half-veiled in the mists,' to David and Solomon, 'round whose lives no mist will ever gather.' With Saul at his side, the old judge presents him under the title 'Anointed of the Eternal.'

Matthew Henry hears in the verse Samuel's own quiet apologia: 'I have walked before you' from childhood, a shepherd leading the flock, and 'now my best days are done: I am old and gray-headed.' The very grievance the people urged against him — his age — should have won him reverence, 'for days shall speak and the multitude of years shall teach wisdom.' Yet Samuel presses no claim; he only notes that he 'hearkened to your voice in all that you said' even 'to the discarding of myself and family,' and asks in return, 'now will you hearken to me?' Across the schools the point converges: the rejected servant, far from sulking, uses the people's very gladness as the doorway to their conscience.
2:
'The king walketh before you' — and the witnesses hear in that walking a whole doctrine of rule. Ellicott takes the image from the shepherd's place 'in the East, where he goes before his flock,' and draws the line straight to the Gospel: as our Lord says, My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me. Henceforth Saul 'must lead, and they must follow'; the days when he 'could quietly betake himself to his old pursuits on the farm of the Ephraim hills were now past for ever.' Wesley and Gill add the personal note: Samuel has 'fully resigned my power' and now 'own myself one of his subjects,' so that no one can suspect him of clinging to office 'with any regret.'

The phrase 'I am old and grayheaded' the commentators read as deliberate pathos. Ellicott notes that Samuel throws the elders' own word back at them — they had pleaded his age at Ramah (chap. 8) — and answers, in effect, 'I am old, grown grey in your service; listen to me while I ask you what manner of service that has been. Can any one find in it a flaw?' Keil and Delitzsch keep close to the Hebrew of the great appeal that follows, glossing the piling of verbs — to defraud, to oppress, to 'pound or crush in pieces' — as the strongest possible denial of injustice.

The hardest phrase is 'my sons are with you,' for these were the very sons whose bribery had triggered the demand for a king (chap. 8). Clarke reads it as a public reckoning: 'these words intimate that Samuel had deprived them of their public employ, and reduced them to a level with the common people.' Wesley agrees — they stand 'among you, in the same states private persons, as you are; if they have injured any of you, the law is now open against them.' Ellicott alone detects a father's ache beneath the surrender: 'possibly a tinge of mortified feeling at the rejection of himself and his family, mixed with a desire to recommend his sons to the favour and goodwill of the nation.' The witnesses thus hold together two truths — the magistrate who abandons his own to justice, and the parent who cannot wholly hide the wound.
3:
'Witness against me' is the verse the witnesses love best, and Clarke gives it his most ringing praise: 'Did ever a minister of state, in any part of the world, resign his office with so much self-consciousness of integrity, backed with the universal approbation of the public? No man was oppressed under his government, no man defrauded!' He had 'accumulated no riches,' provided for no 'needy dependant... out of the public purse.' Clarke even reads the mention of the sons as a double act: 'as a minister of justice, he abandons them to their fate; as a tender father, he indirectly and modestly pleads for them.'

Wesley and Gill insist the protestation is not vanity but strategy. Samuel makes it 'not out of ostentation; but for his own just vindication,' so 'that the people might not hereafter... reproach his government,' and 'that being publickly acquitted from all faults in his government, he might more freely reprove the sins of the people.' A man who would tell another of his sin 'must see to it that he himself be clear.' The ox and the ass, Ellicott explains, are named as the 'representative possessions in this primitive age,' before David and Solomon brought silver and horses; the very things the law of Exodus forbade the Israelite to covet. The 'bribe' is 'literally, ransom,' that Eastern gift of money by which 'a criminal who had means was too often able to escape punishment' — the very charge, we know from chapter 8, laid against Samuel's own sons.

Ellicott lingers longest on 'his anointed.' This, he says, 'is the earliest instance of a king bearing this title of honour,' once given to the high priest, now fixed upon the throne — and its 'infinite charm' flows from the fact that 'one greater than any of the sons of men would, in the far future, assume the same sacred designation, His Anointed, or His Christ.' Ever since Samuel poured the oil on Saul, 'wherever the one true God has been worshipped, an infinite charm has gone with the name.' He even preserves the Rabbis' wonder: the Talmud counted Samuel's tribunal among the three where 'the Holy Spirit manifest Himself,' and cited this very verse to prove 'all the prophets were rich men,' owning their own herds and needing no man's ox. The elders, no respecters of persons, could yet only answer that his 'long official life had been indeed pure and spotless.'
4:
The people's answer is brief, and the witnesses let its brevity ring. 'Thou hast not defrauded us, nor oppressed us, neither hast thou taken ought of any man's hand' — and Clarke can only exclaim, 'Of what minister or governor of any nation under heaven can such things be said?' The very men who had wearied of Samuel, who had pleaded his age and his sons' corruption as their reason for wanting a king, are now 'constrained with one voice' to bear witness that his rule had been flawless.

Keil and Delitzsch catch the irony the acquittal contains. In clearing Samuel so completely, the people 'had condemned themselves,' for 'they had thereby acknowledged on oath that there was no ground for their dissatisfaction with Samuel's administration, and consequently no well-founded reason for their request for a king.' The confession of his innocence is, in the same breath, the confession of their own ingratitude. Gill pictures the reply as spoken 'one in the name of the rest, or they all cried out as one man' — a unanimous verdict, wrung from a crowd that had every motive to find fault and could find none.

Read together, the witnesses turn a single sentence into a mirror. Samuel had asked only for justice at parting, not honour; he 'did not expect that they would do him honour,' as Matthew Henry observes elsewhere, and rehearsed none of his good services, but simply that they should 'do him justice, and that they did.' Henry notes what the people readily owned: that Samuel 'had not made his government oppressive to them, nor used his power to their wrong,' and had 'not made it expensive to them' either — like Nehemiah, who 'did not require the bread of the governor' (Neh. 5:18), he had 'coveted no man's silver, or gold, or apparel' (Acts 20:33). The people's testimony becomes the hinge of the whole chapter: having sworn that their judge had wronged no one, they have removed every excuse, and the prophet is now free to press home that their demand for a king sprang not from any fault in his government but from a fault in their own hearts. The unanimous 'Thou hast not' prepares the ground for the unanimous 'We have sinned' that the thunder will soon draw from them.
5:
Samuel seals the acquittal by summoning heaven itself. 'The Lord is witness against you, and his anointed is witness this day.' Gill notes the forward-looking purpose: the oath binds the people 'should they hereafter reproach and vilify him, and charge him with any acts of corruption, injustice, and violence.' What they have granted freely today they may not retract tomorrow, for God and the king stand as sworn witnesses to their confession.

Keil and Delitzsch press the sharp edge of the word 'against.' Samuel does not say the Lord is witness for him but 'witness against you,' 'because with the declaration which the people had made concerning Samuel's judicial labours they had condemned themselves.' They had 'acknowledged on oath that there was no ground for their dissatisfaction,' and so the very witnesses they call to confirm his innocence become witnesses to their own guilt. Ellicott catches the rising solemnity of the scene: Samuel again, 'with increased solemnity, called the Eternal in the heavens above and His anointed king then standing by his side,' and 'the assembly of Israel, again with one voice, shouted, Yes, He is witness.'

There is a textual delicacy the grammatical readers do not pass over. The Hebrew has a singular 'he said' answering for the whole people, and Keil defends it against the easier plural of the margin, taking 'all Israel' as the subject — the nation speaking as one voice. He adds that this was the very design of the appeal: 'to bring the people to a still more thorough acknowledgment of their sin,' Samuel 'strengthened still more their assent to his solemn appeal to God.' Ellicott records the same double shout of assent, the assembly answering 'again with one voice.' Matthew Henry, commenting on the acquittal, draws the abiding comfort: 'the testimony of our neighbours, and especially the testimony of our own consciences for us, that we have in our places lived honestly, will be our comfort under the slights and contempts that are put upon us' — as Demetrius was 'a happy man, that has a good report of all men and of the truth itself' (3 John 12).

Beneath the legal form the witnesses find a pastoral design: Samuel is not merely protecting his good name but tightening the knot of conviction, so that when he turns from his own record to God's mighty acts, the people cannot plead that they were driven to a king by any grievance. Their own solemn 'He is witness' has cut off that retreat. The stage is set, and the God whom they have twice named as witness will presently answer from the harvest sky.
6:
Having cleared himself, Samuel turns from his own record to God's, and the witnesses agree this is the pivot of the address. Gill says he now lays before them 'some of the great things God had done for them formerly... the more to aggravate their ingratitude in rejecting God as their King.' Matthew Henry expands the method: rather than upbraid the people with their unkindness to himself, Samuel 'sets himself to instruct them,' giving 'an abstract of the history of their nation, that... they might be for ever engaged to love him and serve him.' The work of ministers, Henry adds, 'is to reason with people... to convince men's judgments, and so to gain their wills and affections. Let reason rule men, and they will be good.'

The grammatical-historical readers fasten on a translation crux. Ellicott argues the Hebrew should not read 'It is the Lord that advanced Moses' but 'even the Eternal that advanced Moses and Aaron' — Samuel seizing the people's own cry, 'He is witness,' and answering, yes, the witness is 'the Eternal that made Moses.' The English, he says, 'greatly weakens the dramatic force of the original,' where the Septuagint rightly 'supplied the word witness.' Keil and Delitzsch read it the same way and gloss the verb: God 'made' Moses and Aaron 'in a moral and historical sense... to make a person what he is to be,' their introduction 'upon the stage of history,' like the made faithful of the Epistle — not their physical birth but their calling.

Both Ellicott and Keil preserve the Rabbis' comment that Scripture here 'balances in the same scale the three least important with the three most important personages' — Jerubbaal likened to Moses, Bedan to Aaron, Jephthah to Samuel — 'to teach thee' that 'the most insignificant man, if appointed a ruler of the congregation, has the same authority as the most important personage.' Behind the grammar, the theology is one: the Exodus is named, here and everywhere in these records, as 'the great call of love by which the Eternal assumed the sovereignty over Israel.' The God who raised up Moses and Aaron 'from mean beginnings' is the King they have now, in effect, laid aside — and Samuel means them to feel the weight of what they have exchanged.
7:
'Stand still, that I may reason with you.' Clarke compresses the whole legal drama into a sentence: 'I have arraigned myself before God and you; I now arraign you before God.' The judge who has just submitted to trial now convenes one — but the charges he will read are, paradoxically, a catalogue of kindnesses. Ellicott hears the pathos: 'Samuel proceeds in his painful work... Stand up now, ye elders, while I proceed. My innocence, as your judge, you have thus borne witness to... yet in spite of this you have wished to be quit of me, and of One who stood high above me — of One who has worked for you such mighty deeds, even the Eternal.'

The pivotal phrase is 'the righteous acts of the Lord,' and here the witnesses converge on a fine point of Hebrew. Wesley renders it 'the righteousnesses; that is, mercies or benefits,' noting that in the coming rehearsal 'some of their calamities' are 'but briefly named, and that for the illustration of God's mercy in their deliverances.' Keil and Delitzsch agree that the tsedaqoth are 'the benefits which Jehovah had conferred upon His people, as being the results of His covenant fidelity' — acts that 'attested the righteousness of the Lord in the fulfilment of the covenant grace which He had promised.' God's saving deeds are called righteous because in them He is 'just to his own honour,' faithful to His own word.

Matthew Henry draws the pastoral moral from the mode of address. Samuel bids them 'stand still, stand in token of reverence when God is speaking to you, stand still in token of attention and composedness of mind.' Religion, Henry insists, 'has reason on its side' — he points to the divine invitation, Come now, and let us reason together. The minister's task is not merely 'to exhort and direct, but to persuade, to convince men's judgments, and so to gain their wills and affections.' Gill hears the same summons to patience: 'wait a little longer... and with reverence and attention hearken to what I have further to say.' The witnesses unite in the conviction that God's case against His people is argued, not shouted — that grace itself is the evidence for the prosecution.
8:
The first exhibit in Samuel's case is the Exodus, and the witnesses read it as the foundation-mercy of the nation. Gill traces the arc: Jacob's posterity 'settled in Egypt, and continued there many years, and at length were oppressed... and brought into hard bondage,' until they cried and were redeemed. Clarke distils the pattern that will govern the whole rehearsal: 'through all their history God had ever raised them up deliverers, when their necessities required such interference.' The cry of the oppressed and the sending of a saviour is the rhythm Samuel wants the people to recognize — and to see that they have just broken it by trusting an earthly king instead.

Keil and Delitzsch supply the structure. This is 'the first proof' of God's righteous acts — 'the deliverance of the children of Israel out of Egypt, and their safe guidance into Canaan,' with 'this place' understood as the land of promise. The 'second proof' will follow in the deliverances through the judges, 'as often as they turned to Him with penitence and cried to Him for help.' Ellicott agrees that Samuel 'in order' rehearses 'the deeds of loving-kindness done for Israel by this Eternal King,' beginning with 'the wonders of the Exodus, and how, under that Divine guidance, they were guided through so many dangers safe into the land of Canaan, this place.'

Wesley lingers over a small difficulty: how can Moses and Aaron be said to have brought the people into 'this place,' the land, when neither entered Canaan west of Jordan? He answers that they were 'under God, the principal authors of their entering,' for they 'brought them out of Egypt, conducted them through the wilderness,' preserved them 'by their prayers to God, and counsel to them,' gave 'command from God for the distribution of the land,' and at the last 'Moses substituted Joshua in his stead, and commanded him to seat them there.' The mediators' work reaches beyond their own lifetimes. Across the witnesses the lesson is single and pointed: the same God who heard the groan in Egypt and raised up deliverers is the King these people have grown weary of — and their whole history is the argument against their choice.
9:
Now the rehearsal turns dark: 'when they forgat the Lord their God, he sold them into the hand' of their enemies. Gill defines the forgetting not as a lapse of memory but of worship — 'they fell into idolatry, which is a plain instance and proof of forgetfulness of God; for such as neglect his worship, and serve idols, may be truly said to forget him.' Ellicott sharpens the point: idolatry 'was simply an act of rebellion against the pure government of the invisible King, and was punished by the withdrawal of the Divine protection.' The verb 'sold' is stark; God hands His people over to the very oppressors from whom He had lately saved them.

Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott together identify the three named scourges as 'prominent ones, quoted as they occurred to him, without any careful attention being paid to the order of events and times' — Sisera, 'the well-known commander' of Jabin's host of Hazor; the Philistines; and the king of Moab, whom Ellicott identifies as 'Eglon, who was slain by Ehud.' Of the Philistines Ellicott writes with feeling, comparing their strongholds to 'those robber nests which in the stormy middle ages disturbed the peace' of Europe, and crediting Samuel with first 'really checking these Philistine robbers,' after which Israel's 'advance... was singularly rapid.'

Wesley draws out the argumentative force of the verse, for he sees Samuel answering an unspoken objection. The people might plead that they desired a king because 'in the time of the judges they were at great uncertainties, and often exercised with sharp afflictions.' Wesley grants it — 'by way of concession that they were so' — but adds 'by way of retortion, that they themselves were the cause of it, by their forgetting God: so that it was not the fault of that kind of government, but their transgressing the rules of it.' This is the heart of the chapter's logic. The insecurity that made a king look attractive was self-inflicted; the remedy they craved addressed a symptom while the true disease — forgetfulness of God — would follow them under monarchy as surely as under the judges. The witnesses let the history indict the very demand it seems to explain.
10:
The cycle turns again, and this time toward mercy. 'They cried unto the Lord, and said, We have sinned.' Gill pictures the people 'when in the hands of their enemies, and in bondage to them, and cruelly oppressed' — the extremity that at last wrings out confession. The words are a model of true repentance: they do not merely lament their misery but name its cause, 'because we have forsaken the Lord, and have served Baalim and Ashtaroth,' and they seal the plea with a vow, 'we will serve thee.'

Ellicott dwells on the swiftness of the divine response: 'as soon as they were convinced of their sin and rebellion, and accused themselves, and returned to their old allegiance, their invisible King, ever full of pity and tender compassion, forgave them, and sent them quick deliverance.' The portrait of God here is the one Samuel most wants his hearers to see — not a reluctant or grudging ruler, but a King whose readiness to forgive outruns His people's readiness to repent. That is precisely the King they have exchanged for a man.

On the idols themselves Ellicott turns antiquarian, identifying 'Baal and Ashtaroth' as 'the well-known leading Phoenician deities,' their worship 'imported probably from Carthage, the great Phoenician centre,' where 'the temple of Baal-shemesh, the Sun god... was renowned in that luxurious and splendid city.' Baal and Ashtaroth, 'the Greek Astarte, were probably originally worshipped simply as the sun and moon,' and the plural form 'Baalim,' he notes, 'refers to the various personifications and different titles of the god and goddess.' The very multiplicity of the idols underscores their emptiness — many names, no power. Set beside the single living God who hears one honest cry, the crowded Canaanite pantheon is exposed as the counterfeit it is.

The witnesses read the verse as the hinge of the whole cycle Samuel is tracing: sin brings bondage, bondage brings a cry, the cry brings deliverance. The confession itself is exemplary in its shape — a naming of the specific sin ('we have forsaken the Lord, and have served Baalim and Ashtaroth'), a plea for rescue, and a vow of renewed service. It is the pattern the people know by heart and have just broken, seeking safety from a king rather than from the God who has never once failed to answer 'We have sinned' with a saviour. Samuel rehearses their fathers' repentance, as Gill and Ellicott alike imply, not to shame the children with an old story but to set before them a well-worn road home that is still open.
11:
Samuel names the deliverers — 'Jerubbaal, and Bedan, and Jephthah, and Samuel' — and the witnesses spend most of their energy on the puzzle of Bedan, a name found nowhere else in the book of Judges. Clarke lays out the options: 'instead of Bedan... the Septuagint have Barak; the same reading is found in the Syriac and Arabic. The Targum has Samson,' while 'Calmet thinks that Jair is intended.' He weights the question toward Barak by appeal to the New Testament, since St. Paul lists 'Gideon, of Barak, of Samson, of Jephthah.' Ellicott agrees Barak is most probable, the letters of the two names being 'very similar' so that 'a scribe might easily have written the one for the other,' though he records Kimchi's suggestion that Bedan stands for 'Ben-Dan, the son of Dan,' that is, Samson.

Wesley leans the other way, preferring either Samson — 'called Bedan; that is, in Dan, or of Dan' — or, on grounds of chronology, 'Jair the Gileadite,' since Jair came before Jephthah and Samson after, and since a Bedan appears among the sons of an older Jair in the Chronicles. Ellicott preserves Wordsworth's contrarian charm: better to leave the unknown name standing, for 'in this very obscurity... we have a confirmation of the genuineness of the speech. A forger would not have ventured to insert a name which occurs nowhere else.'

The second difficulty is that Samuel names himself. Ellicott notes the Syriac again substitutes 'Samson for Samuel, finding, doubtless, a difficulty in the quotation of his own name by the speaker.' But he defends the received text: 'in truth Samuel could well cite himself a signal instance of God's loving pity in sending deliverance, conscious as he was of his own high mission. No judge had accomplished such great things for the people, and none had received more general recognition.' Gill keeps it simple, glossing Jerubbaal as Gideon after the Targum. Beneath the tangle of names the point the witnesses will not let slip is the one Wesley states: God 'delivered you... on every side, and ye dwelled safe' — 'so that it was no necessity, but mere wantonness, that made you desire a change.'
12:
Here Samuel reaches the immediate occasion of the whole crisis. 'When ye saw that Nahash the king of the children of Ammon came against you... ye said, Nay; but a king shall reign over us: when the Lord your God was your king.' Ellicott reconstructs the history: 'Nahash and the Ammonites had invaded the trans-Jordanic territory of Israel in the period immediately preceding the demand,' the siege of Jabesh-gilead being 'only one of a series of destructive forays.' The external threat is real; what Samuel indicts is not the danger but the response to it.

Clarke notes that this was 'not the first time they had demanded a king; see before, chap. 8. But at the crisis mentioned here they became more importunate,' and it was 'in consequence of this that the kingdom was a second time confirmed to Saul.' He fixes the constitutional sequence: 'Saul was elected at Mizpeh, he was confirmed at Gilgal.' The Ammonite alarm did not create the demand for a king so much as bring an old wish to a boil. Gill underlines the tragic reflex: instead of 'applying to the Lord for his protection,' at the first sight of Nahash 'they desired to have a king to go before them, and fight their battles.'

Wesley presses the theological scandal home. Israel demanded a captain 'when God was your immediate king and governor, who was both able and willing to deliver you, if you had cried to him,' as 'you and your ancestors have had plentiful experience.' The whole rehearsed history — Egypt, the judges, the deliverances 'on every side' — was proof enough that they 'did not at all need any other king.' Therefore, Wesley concludes, 'your desire of another was a manifest reproach against God,' a public verdict that the living King who had never failed them was somehow not enough. Matthew Henry, on the same words, catches the irony of their timing: they would 'prescribe to God by such a one to save them out of the hand of Nahash,' when Samuel had told them plainly that 'God was commander-in-chief in all their wars and they needed no other.' Yet they insisted, 'Nay, but a king shall reign over us.'

The witnesses converge: the sin lay not in facing Nahash but in facing him without faith, preferring the visible spear of a man to the invisible arm that had scattered every foe before. As Henry frames the paradox that governs the whole chapter, they now have 'a king of your own asking — let that be spoken to your shame; but a king of God's making — let that be spoken to his honour and the glory of his grace.' God 'did not cast them off, even when they in effect cast him off.'
13:
'Behold the king whom ye have chosen, whom ye have asked for! behold, the Lord hath set a king over you.' The witnesses hear two 'beholds' held in tension — the people's choosing and the Lord's setting. Gill resolves them: 'though God chose their king for them, it was at their request; they chose to have a king, and desired one, and they approved of and consented to... the choice he had made, and so it was in effect their own.' Wesley reads the same double authorship: 'though God chose him by lot, yet the people are said to choose him; either generally, because they chose that form of government; or particularly, because they approved of God's choice.' And of the Lord's part he adds, soberly, 'He hath yielded to your inordinate desire.'

Keil and Delitzsch weigh the Hebrew with care. 'By the second behold, the thought is brought out still more strongly, that Jehovah had fulfilled the desire of the people. Although the request of the people had been an act of hostility to God, yet Jehovah had fulfilled it.' Keil notes the word for choosing 'relating to the choice by lot' is set before the word for asking 'to show that the demand was the strongest act that the people could perform. They had not only chosen the king with the consent... of Samuel; they had even demanded a king of their own self-will.'

Ellicott gathers the strands into a single stroke: 'You now have your wish — behold your king. The Eternal has seen fit to grant your petition. His election' — again pointing to Saul — 'rests on the will of the invisible King, whom virtually you have rejected.' The paradox the witnesses will not soften is that the very gift is a judgment: God grants what His people sinfully craved, and in the granting both honours their freedom and exposes their folly. Saul is at once 'a king of your own asking,' as Henry puts it elsewhere, 'and a king of God's making' — the one to their shame, the other to the glory of the grace that would not cast them off even when they had, in effect, cast Him off.
14:
Samuel now turns from the past to the terms of the future, and the witnesses find in this verse a famous grammatical beauty. Ellicott, following Dean Payne Smith, holds that 'the English Version has missed the point.' The Hebrew particle introduces not a completed sentence but an 'ejaculatory' wish: 'Samuel piled up one upon another the conditions of their happiness, and then from the depth of his emotion breaks off, leaving the blessed consequences of their obedience unsaid.' The heart of it is a cry: 'O that you would only fear the Lord! O that you and your king would only continue following!' Ellicott compares the broken sentence of our Lord over Jerusalem, If thou hadst known.

Keil and Delitzsch confirm the reading from the Hebrew, taking the conditional particle 'in the sense of modo, if only,' virtually equivalent to the wish 'O that ye would only!' They defend the phrase 'be after the Lord' as good Hebrew for 'attaching one's self' to Him, following as a subject follows his king. Wesley presses the same phrase to a fine pastoral distinction: 'to go after the Lord' here 'notes not a duty to be performed, but a privilege to be received upon the performance of their duty.' That is, God 'shall still go before you, as he hath hitherto done, as your leader or governor, to direct, protect, and deliver you' — the obedience is the condition, but the continued divine leadership is the reward.

Matthew Henry draws out the sweetness of that arrangement. 'Following God is a work that is its own wages. It is the matter of a promise as well as of a precept.' To those sincere in religion 'God will give grace to persevere,' so that 'those that follow God faithfully will be divinely strengthened to continue following him.' Clarke and Gill keep the condition plain — 'on condition that ye rebel no more, God will take you and your king under his merciful protection' — and Gill roots the whole in reverence, since 'all worship and service of God... should spring from fear.' The witnesses agree that Samuel's mercy here outstrips his warning: even a nation that has just sinned in demanding a king may yet, king and all, walk safely behind the Lord if only it will.
15:
The blessing has its shadow: 'if ye will not obey the voice of the Lord, but rebel... then shall the hand of the Lord be against you, as it was against your fathers.' Keil and Delitzsch attend to the small word rendered 'as,' the Hebrew waw 'used in a comparative sense,' so that the clause means 'as it was upon your fathers.' The reference to the fathers, Keil observes, 'is very suitable here, because the people were looking to the king for the removal of all the calamities, which had fallen upon them from time immemorial.' A king, in other words, is no shield against the hand of God; the same judgments that fell on their idolatrous ancestors will fall on them if they follow the same path.

The grammatical-historical readers note a textual variant here. Keil rejects the Septuagint's paraphrase 'against your king,' and Ellicott records that 'Rabbi D. Kimchi prefers to understand fathers as put for kings: the hand of the Lord shall be against you and your kings,' while 'the LXX. reads, against you and your king.' Ellicott himself, with most, keeps the plain sense, that the English translation 'accurately and happily understands the Hebrew in the sense of as: as it was against your fathers.' The comparison to the fathers is the point Samuel wants felt.

Wesley and Gill draw the moral straight. Sinning 'they and their king,' Israel 'shall have no advantage by the change of government, nor shall your kings be able to protect you against God's displeasure.' Wesley states the maxim that Matthew Henry elsewhere sharpens to an aphorism: it is a mistake 'if we think we can evade God's justice, by shaking off his dominion. If we will not let God rule us, yet he will judge us.' Here the witnesses expose the deepest illusion behind the demand for a king — the hope that a human government might buy exemption from divine accountability. The monarchy changes the form of Israel's life but not its terms: obedience is still the only safety, and no crown can stand between a rebellious people and the hand that once bruised their fathers.
16:
'Stand and see this great thing.' Samuel had earlier said 'stand and hear' (v. 7); now, Matthew Henry notes, 'because he did not see that his reasoning with them affected them... he bids them stand and see.' If the still, small voice of argument would not reach their hearts, 'they shall hear God speaking to them in dreadful claps of thunder.' Henry insists the wonder was a true miracle, not merely a well-timed storm: it came 'in an unusual time, in wheat-harvest, and this on a fair clear day, when there appeared not to the eye any signs of a storm,' and — most tellingly — Samuel gave 'notice of it before,' so that its arrival 'proved him a true prophet.'

Keil and Delitzsch fasten on the emphatic Hebrew, gam-attah, 'yea, even now,' which governs the whole appeal: 'Even now come hither, and see this great thing which Jehovah does before your eyes.' The interposed word 'place yourselves' — make yourselves ready — is set in 'to fix the attention of the people more closely upon the following miracle, as an event of great importance.' Clarke is content to gloss it 'this unusual occurrence,' while Gill notes that to 'stand' here 'does not so much respect the position of their bodies as the fixed attention of their minds.'

Henry reads the theology of the thunder in three strokes. First, God 'testified his displeasure against them in the same way in which he had formerly testified it' — the very weapon of the great thunder that had once routed the Philistines is now turned against rebellious Israel, as when the Lord 'turned to be their enemy.' Second, it showed 'their folly in desiring a king to save them, rather than God': could their king 'thunder with a voice like God' (Job)? Could their prince 'command such forces as the prophet could by his prayers?' Third, it warned that however serene their new estate seemed, 'like the weather in wheat-harvest,' God 'could soon change the face of their heavens.' Ellicott lifts the whole to its height: their 'wishing for an earthly king was the crown of a long course of rebellion... the breaking up for ever of the glorious ideal which had been for so long before the eyes of the noblest spirits in Israel.'
17:
'Is it not wheat harvest today?' The force of the sign, all the witnesses agree, lies wholly in the season. Gill assembles the evidence: rain in Judea 'fell only twice a year, called the former and the latter rain,' and 'from... March to... October, it was not usual for rain to fall.' He cites 'R. Joseph Kimchi' that 'in the land of Israel rain never fell all the days of harvest,' 'confirmed by Jerome, who lived long in those parts.' Clarke quotes Jerome directly — that in Judea, at the end of June and in July, 'we never saw rain' — adding that what happened now 'hardly ever occurs there but in the winter months.' Ellicott sets the harvest 'between the middle of May and the middle of June,' when 'rain in that season seldom or never falls, but if it does it is usually severe.'

Gill presses a further detail overlooked by the others: on that very day 'the men were at work in the fields reaping the wheat,' and so the sky was 'all serene and clear,' 'or otherwise they would not have been employed in cutting down the corn.' The clearness of the morning made the storm the more unanswerable — no cloud had warned of it, and the reapers themselves were unwitting proof that the heavens gave no natural sign. Keil and Delitzsch simply record the demand and its purpose: 'that ye may perceive and see that your wickedness is great... in asking you a king.'

Wesley gathers the meaning. The rain was sent 'that you may understand that God is displeased with you; and also how foolishly and wickedly you have done in rejecting the government of that God, at whose command are all things both in heaven and in earth.' The sign is a sermon in the sky: the God the people had, in effect, dismissed as insufficient to fight their battles now demonstrates that the very weather obeys His word at the prayer of His servant. The witnesses read the harvest thunder as an argument the dullest conscience could not evade — for a people who wanted a king to save them, here is the King of the seasons overturning the settled order of nature to press home a single, humbling truth: their wickedness was great in the sight of the Lord.
18:
'So Samuel called unto the Lord; and the Lord sent thunder and rain that day.' The witnesses first make sure of the manner of the calling. Gill insists it was 'not in an authoritative way, or by way of command, but by prayer,' pointing to the Targum's rendering of the previous verse, 'I will pray before the Lord.' The prophet does not summon the storm as a magician might; he asks, and God answers. Clarke stresses the completeness of the wonder: the thunder and rain were 'totally unusual; and, as it came at the call of Samuel, was a most evident miracle.'

The effect is instant terror. 'All the people greatly feared the Lord and Samuel.' Clarke distinguishes the two fears: 'they dreaded His terrible majesty; and they feared Samuel, perceiving that he had so much power with God.' The nation that days before had rejoiced over its new king now trembles before the older authority it had slighted — the God who thunders and the prophet whose prayer is heard. Matthew Henry, on the same scene, marks the reversal with a pointed phrase: 'now they were rejoicing in their king, God taught them to rejoice with trembling.' When they had a king 'they were ready to think they must fear him only,' but God 'made them know that he is greatly to be feared and his prophets for his sake.'

Wesley draws the contrast the whole sign was built to make. By this thunder God 'shewed them their folly in desiring a king to save them, rather than God or Samuel, expecting more from an arm of flesh than from the arm of God, or from the power of prayer. Could their king thunder with a voice like God? Could their prince command such forces as the prophet could by his prayers?' And he adds the sober undertone that Samuel means them to carry home: 'how serene soever their condition was now, like the weather in wheat harvest, yet if God pleased, he could soon change the face of their heavens, and persecute them with his storms.' The witnesses land together on the lesson: the true power in Israel was never the sceptre but the God who answers prayer, and one honest cry from a righteous man moved the heavens that no king could command.
19:
The thunder does its work, and the people cry, 'Pray for thy servants... that we die not: for we have added unto all our sins this evil, to ask us a king.' Gill pictures the confession wrung out 'during the tempest, and in the midst of it,' as 'the general cry of the people... unanimous.' Keil and Delitzsch mark the terror as 'salutary' — the miracle 'inspired the people with a salutary terror' so that they entreat the prophet whom lately they had set aside.

Clarke draws a careful theological line the panic must not blur: 'It is no sin to have a king; a good king is one of the greatest blessings of God's providence; but it is a sin to put a man in the place of God.' Their fault was never the institution but the motive — trusting the visible ruler as though the invisible King were insufficient. Yet Clarke turns the moment into a searching rebuke of half-repentance: 'Is it not strange that they did not now attempt to repair their fault? They might have done it, but they did not; they acknowledged their sin, but did not put it away. This is the general way of mankind' — content 'to bear the character' of sinner, 'heedless of the consequences.'

Matthew Henry hears in the appeal a portrait of every reluctant believer. The people 'earnestly begged Samuel's prayers... Now they see their need of him whom awhile ago they slighted. Thus many that will not have Christ to reign over them would yet be glad to have him intercede for them, to turn away the wrath of God.' The verse becomes a mirror for the human heart, which resists the rule of the very One whose mediation it is glad to claim in the hour of fear. Wesley catches the pathos in their choice of words, 'thy God' — they ask Samuel to pray to the Lord as his God, 'while we are ashamed and afraid to call him our God.' The witnesses agree the fear is real and even wholesome, yet Clarke's warning stands over it: acknowledgment is not amendment, and a repentance that names the sin without forsaking it leaves the heart where it was.
20:
'Fear not: ye have done all this wickedness; yet turn not aside from following the Lord.' The witnesses hear in this the most evangelical note of the chapter. Ellicott calls it 'a very great and precious evangelical truth,' showing 'how deeply this eminent servant of the Most High had entered into the Eternal thought. No sin or course of sin was too great to be repented of.' Afar off, he says, these ministers 'saw, though perhaps in a glass darkly, the Lamb of God, whose blood cleanseth from all sin.' He sets Samuel's word in a great chorus: with Isaiah's 'though your sins be as scarlet, they shall be white as snow,' with Hosea's 'O Israel, return unto the Lord thy God,' and with Paul's summons to press on, 'forgetting all the things that were behind.'

Clarke catches the concessive grammar exactly: the sense is 'although ye have done all this wickedness, what was past God would pass by, provided they would be obedient in future.' The 'Fear not' does not deny the guilt but refuses to let the guilt become despair. Wesley makes the same distinction with precision: 'Fear not with a desponding fear, as if there were no hope left for you.' The dread that the thunder raised was meant to drive them to God, not away from Him.

Matthew Henry states the pastoral logic plainly: 'the terrors of the Lord' were 'intended to frighten them to him' — 'the weather will clear up after the storm.' A people crushed by conviction might have concluded that God was done with them; Samuel forbids the thought. Every transgression, Henry says elsewhere, 'though it displease the Lord, yet does not throw us out of covenant.' Gill keeps the counsel simple — 'fear not' 'being destroyed by the tempest.' The witnesses unite in a truth larger than the moment: the same voice of God that terrifies the sinner also forbids his despair, and the door left open after the thunder is the door of return. Samuel will not let the storm end in a heap of frightened consciences; he turns their fear into the first step of following.
21:
'Turn not aside after vain things, which cannot profit nor deliver; for they are vain.' The witnesses hear Samuel naming the real alternative to following God — not neutrality but idolatry. Clarke reaches for the language of creation: the 'vain things' are idols, 'which he calls here hattohu, the same expression found Genesis. The earth was tohu; it was waste, empty, and formless: so idols; they are confusion, and things of naught, for an idol is nothing in the world.' Wesley gathers the biblical verdict on them — 'so idols are called' in Deuteronomy and Jeremiah — 'and so they are, being mere nothings, having no power in them; no influence upon us, nor use or benefit to us.' Keil and Delitzsch read the warning as the negative face of the promise: to turn from the Lord is inevitably to 'fall away to idols, which could not bring them either help or deliverance.'

The grammatical-historical readers pause on a textual nicety. Ellicott observes that the passage 'is more forcible without the for and the words in italics supplied in the English translation,' the verse reading simply 'Turn ye not aside after vain things which cannot profit.' Strikingly, he notes, 'not one of the ancient versions translate the Hebrew ki, for: they all omit it,' so that in his judgment the little word 'has, through some copyist's error, got into the text since the versions were made.' The emendation, if right, only tightens the sentence into a single clean prohibition.

Gill keeps the pastoral edge, warning that if they 'turn ye not aside... from his worship,' well; but if they do, they embrace what can only fail them. The witnesses converge on a doctrine of idolatry that is really a doctrine of emptiness: whatever a soul sets in God's place, it sets its hope on 'nothing in the world.' Matthew Henry, commenting nearby, presses it into a proverb — 'whatever we make a god of, we shall find it deceive us. Creatures in their own places are good; but when put in God's place, they are vain things.' The verse thus turns the people's whole crisis into a single choice: the living God who answers from the harvest sky, or the empty tohu that can neither profit nor deliver.
22:
'The Lord will not forsake his people for his great name's sake.' The witnesses find here the deep ground of Israel's security, and they agree it lies not in the people but in God. Gill and Clarke stress the motive: should God forsake them, His 'name would be blasphemed among the Heathens,' charged 'with want of power to help them, or with want of faithfulness to his promise.' Clarke is blunt that the ground is not their merit: 'it was not however for their sake that he would not cast them off, but for his own great name's sake. He drew his reasons from himself.' Keil and Delitzsch anchor the phrase in Israel's history: God will preserve His people 'for the great name which He had acquired in the sight of all the nations, by the marvellous guidance of Israel thus far,' guarding it 'against misapprehension and blasphemy,' as at Joshua.

The second clause — 'because it hath pleased the Lord to make you his people' — draws the grammatical-historical Ellicott to the doctrine of election, which he states with unusual candour: 'the simple doctrine of election — as far as we can see, based alone on the arbitrary will of God (though, no doubt, unseen by us, deep reasons exist for every seemingly arbitrary choice) — is here enunciated.' He appeals to 'the analogy of every-day life,' where God 'maketh one vessel to honour and another to dishonour,' confessing 'these things are to us inscrutable.'

Wesley presses the pastoral use of the truth. God alleges His own name and pleasure 'to take them off from all conceit of their own merit; and to assure them, that if they did truly repent... yet even in that case their salvation would not be due to their merits; but the effect of God's free mercy.' God made them His people 'out of his own free grace, without any desert of yours, and therefore he will not forsake you, except you thrust him away.' The witnesses stand together on a comfort that is also a rebuke of pride: the fixedness of God's choice, as Henry puts it nearby, 'is owing to the freeness of it' — 'choosing them for his name's sake, for his name's sake he will not leave them.' The very freeness that gives the promise its stability strips the people of any claim to have earned it.
23:
'God forbid that I should sin against the Lord in ceasing to pray for you.' The witnesses are moved by the magnanimity of the reply. Clarke notes the people had begged Samuel to pray 'that they might not die,' and 'he tells them that he should consider himself a sinner, should he cease to be their intercessor.' The judge whom they rejected answers their fear not with reproach but with a vow of unbroken prayer — and, more, with the promise 'I will teach you the good and the right way,' which Clarke unfolds warmly: 'It is right — there is no crookedness in it; it is good — there is no evil in it.'

Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott both preserve the comment of the Berleburg Bible, that here Samuel 'sets a glorious example to all rulers, showing them that they should not be led astray by the ingratitude of their subordinates or subjects, and give up on that account all interest in their welfare, but should rather persevere all the more in their anxiety for them.' Ellicott adds Gregory's observation that Jeremiah names Moses and Samuel together as men of 'extraordinary power' with God, 'and why? because they prayed for their enemies.' Samuel's impassioned 'Pray for you!' is the very cry of intercession that will not be stopped by rejection.

Ellicott also traces the change in Samuel's office that the verse quietly registers. With a king now reigning, 'such authority as he had wielded as Judge... must fall into abeyance. But one, and that a still higher office, still remained to him untouched... that of prophet.' In this sphere Samuel 'would work ceaselessly on,' and his words 'tell out to all ages that the true function of the prophet or the preacher of the Eternal is to teach the people the good and the right way.' Ellicott quotes Ewald's tribute: 'Only a Samuel could thus quit office, proudly challenging all to convict him of one single injustice... and by the act of resignation gaining, not losing, greatness.' Gill adds that Samuel forgave their offence against himself as freely as he trusted God to forgive theirs, promising 'all the good offices for them that lay in his power.' The witnesses see in the deposed judge a picture of the truest greatness — the servant who, stripped of authority, keeps every duty of love.
24:
'Only fear the Lord, and serve him in truth with all your heart.' The witnesses read the little word 'only' as gathering the whole duty of Israel into one. Gill is careful about the kind of fear commanded: 'not with a servile fear, which is before dehorted from, but with a filial fear, a reverential affection for God,' a fear that 'includes the whole of religious worship, internal and external.' Clarke unfolds each phrase like a catechism: to fear is to 'know, respect, and reverence him'; to serve 'in truth' is to 'be ever honest, ever sincere'; to serve 'with all your heart' is to 'have every affection engaged,' acting 'not merely from a principle of duty, but also from a pious, affectionate sense of obligation.' In a memorable image he bids them 'act towards your God as an affectionate child should act towards a tender and loving parent.'

The motive Samuel urges is gratitude: 'consider how great things he hath done for you.' Keil and Delitzsch pause on precisely what those great things are — not, in the first place, 'causing it to thunder and rain at Samuel's prayer, but by giving them a king.' The very concession the whole chapter has treated as their sin is now named among God's mercies, for even in granting their faithless request God was doing them good. Clarke widens the gaze: 'Review the history of your fathers, review your own life; see what interpositions of power, mercy, goodness, and truth, God has displayed in your behalf! Has he not daily loaded you with his benefits?'

Wesley keeps the sober condition ringing under the exhortation: 'Only... otherwise neither my prayer nor counsels will stand you in any stead.' Samuel's intercession, promised so freely in the verse before, is no substitute for the people's own sincerity; prayer and teaching profit nothing where the heart is divided. The witnesses unite the two halves of the verse into a single pastoral logic — remembrance of mercy is the spring of obedience, and obedience 'with all your heart' the only fitting return. To fear God as a child fears a loving parent, and to serve Him because He has 'daily loaded you with his benefits,' is the whole of the religion Samuel leaves behind him as his parting charge.
25:
The chapter ends where mercy and warning meet: 'But if ye shall still do wickedly, ye shall be consumed, both ye and your king.' Gill defines the 'still do wickedly' as persistence — to 'continue to rebel against God, revolt from him, and depart from his worship, and despise his prophets, and serve idols.' The threat is not against a single lapse but against a settled course; Samuel's last word leaves the door of return open even as it names the ruin beyond it.

Clarke hears in the verse an echo of the great covenant choice set before Israel of old: 'Here they had set before them life and good, death and evil.' It is the language of the Deuteronomic blessing and curse, now pressed upon a people at the threshold of monarchy. And he draws the sobering historical retrospect: 'Never was a people more fully warned, and never did a people profit less by the warning; and they continue to this day monuments of God's justice and forbearance.' The 'both ye and your king' is pointed — the crown they had craved as their security would share their destruction if they persisted in sin. No institution, however desired, can outlast disobedience to God.

Keil and Delitzsch let the verse stand in its bare severity, the closing counterpart to the promise of verse 14: obedience means king and people continuing after the Lord, rebellion means king and people consumed together. Clarke turns the whole at the last from history to the reader's own conscience: 'Reader, What art thou? Perhaps a similar monument. Consider therefore what great things God has done for thee.' The witnesses close the chapter as Samuel closed his address — not with despair but with a faithful watchman's warning, having, in Henry's phrase from the same passage, 'delivered his own soul.' The alternatives could not be plainer: to fear the Lord and live, or to do wickedly and be consumed, king and people alike. The mercy that hedged the warning all through the chapter still stands; but so does the warning, and Israel's later history would prove how gravely it was meant.
